economic_hypothesis: Static role weights fail because a sleeve's account value is conditional on state: while target distance is large and the full MLL buffer remains, bounded alpha concurrency can accelerate positive expectancy; after losses, buffer depletion or a large daily gain, routing toward stabilizing roles or inactivity should preserve the same accumulated expectancy better than a role-permuted control.
who_pays: The proposed edge remains the frozen development sleeve economics; the router claims only that scarce account risk is wasted when identical sleeves are treated uniformly across high-buffer and defensive account states.
why_it_may_persist: Topstep target, MLL and consistency constraints create stable account-state asymmetry: the marginal value of another unit of exposure differs when the account has a full buffer, is near its MLL floor, or has already concentrated profit in the current day.
CONTRE: The frozen component roles were assigned from development evidence and may not carry causal information. A state router also adds complexity and can manufacture target progress by taking more risk; same-membership role-permutation controls and explicit MLL/consistency non-deterioration are therefore mandatory.
